title: The Social Contract
summary: The political theory that legitimate government rests on agreement — explicit or implicit — among the people it governs, developed by Hobbes, Locke, and Rousseau into the foundational framework of modern constitutional democracy, and contested ever since on questions of who actually agreed, what was agreed to, and whether the theory can justify political obligation at all.
hook: You never signed a contract with your government. Neither did your parents, or their parents. Yet social contract theory — the idea that political authority rests on the consent of the governed — is the foundational principle of modern democratic legitimacy. Every constitution that derives authority 'from the people,' every revolution justified by governmental breach of trust, every argument that citizens have a right to resist tyranny — all trace back to this idea. Understanding social contract theory is understanding why modern governments claim the right to rule, and why that claim has always been contested.

beginner.theConcept:
Why should anyone obey the government?

One powerful answer: because they agreed to. Not individually, not by signing anything — but in the sense that the government's authority rests on the consent of the people it governs. A government that violates that agreement loses its right to obedience. A government that was never agreed to has no legitimate authority at all.

This is the social contract. It is not a historical event. No ancient document records people gathering to create their first government. It is a way of thinking about what makes political authority legitimate — and what makes it not.

The idea became especially important in the 17th century, when religious civil wars across Europe had destroyed the old basis for political authority. Kings had traditionally claimed to rule by God's appointment. After decades of bloody conflict over which God and which king, that answer was no longer convincing. A new basis for legitimate government was needed. Social contract theory provided one.
beginner.problemItAddresses:
The social contract solves a specific problem: obedience is not the same as obligation.

A powerful criminal organization can force people to obey. That does not make it a legitimate government. Force alone cannot explain why people are morally required to follow the law. Something more is needed.

Social contract theory says that something more is consent — or at least the kind of agreement that rational people would reach. Governments are legitimate when the people they govern have agreed to them, or when they are structured in a way that reasonable people would accept.

Before this answer existed, the main alternative was divine authority. Rulers ruled because God appointed them. Social contract theory replaced this with a human-centered foundation. It said political authority rests on persons, not on divine mandate. This was a significant shift with lasting consequences.
beginner.competingTraditions:
The three great theorists of the social contract imagined it very differently.

Hobbes, writing in the 17th century, began from a bleak picture. Without government, life is a war of all against all — violent, frightened, and short. People agree to submit to an absolute ruler just to escape this nightmare. On Hobbes's account, almost any government is better than none. Disobedience is barely ever justified.

Locke drew a milder picture. People in the natural state already have rights — to life, liberty, and property. The contract is made to protect these rights better than individuals can protect them alone. A government that systematically violates those rights has broken the contract. People may dissolve it and start again. This logic justified the American Revolution.

Rousseau argued that the only truly legitimate authority is the collective self-governance of the people themselves. Real freedom is not freedom from government — it is participation in governing yourself. A contract that trades freedom for security misses the point. What matters is who governs and whether governance expresses the genuine will of all.
beginner.politicalStakes:
The social contract is now built into the foundations of modern political life.

Every constitution that begins "We the People" is invoking the social contract. Every declaration of independence that accuses a government of violating the rights of the governed is invoking it. The idea that political authority requires consent has become so fundamental that it is often invisible — absorbed into assumptions so basic they go unquestioned.

But the theory has always carried a hidden exclusion. Hobbes, Locke, and Rousseau imagined their contracts made among men — specifically, among propertied men. Women were not parties. Enslaved people were not parties. Colonized peoples were not parties.

Critics have argued that this exclusion was not accidental. The "social contract" was not a universal agreement among all people — it was a specific arrangement among a specific group. Its promises of equality and freedom coexisted with slavery, colonialism, and the legal subordination of women for centuries. Whether those arrangements were a failure to apply the contract's logic consistently — or whether they were part of what the contract actually established — is one of the deepest debates in political theory.
beginner.contestedHistoryC:
The 20th century produced a new way of using the social contract — not to ask whether past governments were legitimate, but to design principles of justice from scratch.

The key move: imagine that you are choosing the principles that will govern your society, but you don't know where you will end up in it. You don't know if you'll be rich or poor, male or female, majority or minority, healthy or ill. From behind this "veil of ignorance," what principles would you choose?

This thought experiment shifts the social contract from a historical claim to a philosophical tool. The question is no longer "did people actually consent to this government?" but "what arrangements would rational people agree to if they didn't know their own position?"

The answer tends toward fairness. Principles chosen without knowing your starting position will be more egalitarian than principles chosen by people who know they will benefit from existing arrangements. This made the method influential for thinking about justice, not just legitimacy.
beginner.whereDebateStands:
Contemporary social contract thinking has split into two directions, which agree on the form but disagree about the content.

Contractarianism asks: what would self-interested, rational people agree to? The point is not to appeal to morality but to ground morality — to show that even purely self-interested people would accept certain rules because cooperation benefits everyone.

Contractualism asks a different question: what principles could be justified to everyone affected by them? The point is not self-interest but mutual respect — the idea that we owe each other reasons for how we treat each other. To act morally is to act on principles that you could justify to the people they affect.

Both traditions are trying to answer the same underlying question: how do you justify political and moral obligations to people who never actually agreed to them? The answer shapes everything from tax policy to international law to the treatment of future generations.

general.theConcept:
Social contract theory was not written in a library. Each major theorist was responding to a specific political crisis.

Hobbes wrote during the English Civil War, when the breakdown of political order seemed to confirm his account of what life without authority looks like. Locke wrote as a justification for resistance to Charles II and James II. Rousseau wrote against French absolute monarchy. Each theory is a response to a crisis, and understanding the crisis illuminates the theory.

This context explains why social contract theory became so influential in the 17th and 18th centuries. Traditional sources of political legitimacy — divine right, inherited custom, religious authority — had been shattered by religious civil wars. A new basis for political authority was needed, one that didn't depend on any particular theological tradition. Social contract theory provided it: authority derives from rational agreement among persons.
general.problemItAddresses:
The state of nature — the thought experiment imagining life without political institutions — has been criticized from multiple directions.

Empirically: humans have never lived outside social groups. The state of nature is fiction, not prehistory. Philosophically: the individuals imagined in the state of nature already possess rights, reason, and property — they are already political persons in disguise. Politically: each theorist's state of nature conveniently supports the political conclusion they want. Hobbes's brutal state of nature supports absolute sovereignty. Locke's mild state of nature supports limited government. Rousseau's corrupted state supports egalitarianism.

The strongest version of this critique: the state of nature doesn't describe human nature — it describes the political assumptions the theorist brought to the thought experiment. If that's right, the conclusions drawn from it carry no independent weight.
general.competingTraditions:
Hume's critique of social contract theory is the most important early objection. No actual contract was ever made. Most people were born into their political societies without being asked. Authority rests on habit and utility, not on agreement.

Hume's alternative: political obligation follows from the utility of political institutions, not from consent. We obey governments because those institutions produce the stable political order that benefits everyone, not because we agreed to them.

Contract theorists have typically responded in two ways. First, tacit consent: by choosing to remain in a society rather than leave it, you implicitly accept its authority. Second, hypothetical consent: what matters is not whether you actually agreed but whether you would agree under the right conditions. Neither response fully satisfies Hume's objection. The utilitarian challenge to contract theory remains live.
general.politicalStakes: Feminist political theory's engagement with social contract theory has been one of its most productive developments. Carole Pateman's The Sexual Contract (1988) argued that the modern social contract is not a universal agreement among individuals but a fraternal pact among men that explicitly establishes women's subordination in the private sphere. The public sphere of equal citizenship is built on a private sphere of patriarchal power. This argument has been enormously influential in feminist political theory and has generated a substantial literature on the gendered assumptions embedded in liberal political thought.
general.contestedHistoryC:
If the social contract excluded women by design, it excluded people of color by the same logic.

The claim: the actual social contract of Western liberal societies has been an agreement among white people to maintain racial hierarchy while publicly claiming universal principles. The ideals of equality and freedom articulated in liberal political theory coexisted with slavery, colonialism, and racial segregation for centuries. On this account, that coexistence is not a failure to apply liberalism's principles consistently. It is a structural feature of what the contract actually established.

This argument challenges the standard story in which liberal political theory produced ideals that were imperfectly realized over time. Instead, the imperfect realization was the point — the formal ideology of universal rights provided cover for a real arrangement that distributed benefits and burdens along racial lines. This critique has become central to both critical race theory and political philosophy.
general.whereDebateStands:
The most important recent shift in contract theory moves the question from political authority to moral obligation.

The older question was: why must citizens obey the government? The newer question is: what do people owe each other simply as members of a moral community?

One approach grounds the answer in rational self-interest. Even purely self-interested people would agree to moral constraints, because cooperation produces benefits unavailable through defection. Morality, on this account, is what rational bargainers would accept.

A different approach grounds the answer in mutual justifiability. To act morally is to act on principles you could justify to the people they affect. The question is not what self-interested persons would agree to — it is what principles no one could reasonably reject. This connects social contract thinking to a basic account of what we owe each other as persons.

advanced.theConcept: The problem of political obligation — why citizens are morally required to obey political authority — has been the central question addressed by social contract theory and the question that has most resisted satisfactory answers. A.J. Simmons's Moral Principles and Political Obligations (1979) argued that most people in most states are simply not politically obligated — that neither actual consent, tacit consent, fair play, nor natural duty provides a compelling general account of why citizens must obey their governments. This 'philosophical anarchist' position — accepting that there is no general obligation to obey the law while still complying for prudential and other reasons — has been influential and disturbing in equal measure.
advanced.problemItAddresses: The relationship between social contract theory and international order — whether a contract theory can justify obligations beyond the nation-state — has been addressed by Rawls (in The Law of Peoples), by cosmopolitan liberals (Pogge, Beitz), and by communitarians. Rawls's Law of Peoples extends a social contract approach to international relations but reaches conservative conclusions: international justice requires decent treatment of citizens by their governments and assistance to 'burdened societies,' but not global redistribution. Cosmopolitan critics argue that if the veil of ignorance is the right procedure, it should extend to ignorance of nationality — which would produce much more demanding global justice requirements.
advanced.competingTraditions: David Gauthier's Morals by Agreement (1986) attempts to derive morality entirely from rational self-interest — to show that even purely self-interested persons would agree to moral constraints because cooperation produces benefits unavailable through defection. This project in contractarianism — grounding morality in rational bargaining — has been extensively criticized: critics argue that the rationality assumptions are implausible, that the theory cannot generate obligations to those outside the bargaining process (the severely disabled, non-human animals, future generations), and that morality cannot be grounded in self-interest without losing its distinctively moral character.
advanced.politicalStakes: The Rawlsian public reason tradition — the argument that in a pluralist democracy, political decisions should be justified in terms that citizens with different comprehensive doctrines could all accept — has generated one of the most active debates in contemporary political philosophy. Jürgen Habermas's discourse ethics provides an alternative contractualist account based on ideal communication rather than hypothetical choice. John Finnis and other natural law theorists argue that political justification requires substantive moral truth rather than the procedural neutrality that public reason advocates. The debate about what kinds of reasons are appropriate for democratic politics is partly a debate about the right form of social contract theory.
advanced.contestedHistoryC: The debate between historical contractarianism (Locke, the American founders: governments derive authority from actual historical consent) and hypothetical contractarianism (Kant, Rawls: governments should be structured as if they had been consented to by rational persons) has practical political consequences. Historical contractarianism can ground a right of revolution — if the original contract has been broken, the people may dissolve the government. Hypothetical contractarianism cannot ground a right of revolution — hypothetical consent does not create actual obligations or actual rights. Simmons has argued that the shift from historical to hypothetical contractarianism in modern liberal theory has obscured the radical political implications of the original contract tradition.
advanced.whereDebateStands: Recent work by political theorists including Tommie Shelby and Thomas Pogge has applied social contract theory to questions of racial justice and global poverty with results that challenge both the mainstream Rawlsian tradition and its critics. Shelby's We Who Are Dark (2005) uses contractualist reasoning to analyze the obligations of African Americans under conditions of racial injustice — asking what principles governing civic obligations could be justified to people living in conditions of systematic racial disadvantage. This work shows that social contract theory, applied honestly to the conditions in which most people actually live, produces more radical conclusions than its mainstream defenders have acknowledged.

foundation.theStory: The social contract matters as the foundational theory of modern democratic legitimacy — the idea that political authority rests on consent rather than divine right or force. Understanding it requires engaging with its three great formulations (Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau), its contemporary revival (Rawls), and the critiques that have shown its historical exclusions (Pateman, Mills).
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern whether consent — actual or hypothetical — can ground political obligation, whether the 'state of nature' thought experiment illuminates or obscures the foundations of political authority, the feminist and racial critiques of the theory's exclusions, and whether social contract thinking can be extended to global justice.
foundation.whyItMatters: The social contract matters for understanding Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau, Rawls, the American and French revolutions, constitutional democracy, feminist political theory, critical race theory, and contemporary debates about political obligation and global justice.
questions: Hobbes, Locke, and Rousseau all use the 'state of nature' thought experiment but reach radically different conclusions — Hobbes to absolute sovereignty, Locke to limited government with a right of revolution, Rousseau to direct democracy and the general will. How can the same thought experiment support such different conclusions? What does this tell us about the relationship between philosophical premises and political conclusions?

Carole Pateman argued that the social contract is a sexual contract — an agreement among men that establishes patriarchal power over women in the private sphere while claiming equal citizenship in the public sphere. Charles Mills argued it is a racial contract — an agreement among white people to maintain racial hierarchy. If these critiques are correct, what follows for the legitimacy of the political institutions that the social contract tradition has justified?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The essential primary texts are Hobbes's Leviathan (especially Parts I and II), Locke's Second Treatise, Rousseau's Social Contract, and Rawls's A Theory of Justice. Carole Pateman's The Sexual Contract and Charles Mills's The Racial Contract are the essential critical accounts. T.M. Scanlon's What We Owe to Each Other provides the best contemporary contractualist alternative. A.J. Simmons's work provides the most rigorous treatment of political obligation.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: Social contract theory is primarily a normative philosophical project — it argues about what would justify political authority, not about how authority actually developed. The main limitation is that hypothetical consent ('what rational persons would agree to') is a different and weaker basis for obligation than actual consent. The theory's political implications depend heavily on what the hypothetical contractors know and what they are choosing among — different specifications produce radically different conclusions.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about the social contract must engage with the consent problem — how hypothetical or tacit consent can ground actual political obligations. It must address the exclusion problem — whether the feminist and racial critiques show that social contract theory is structurally biased or merely historically misapplied. And it must engage with the global justice question — whether Rawlsian contractualism should extend to international institutions.
questions: Rawls used the social contract tradition to argue for principles of justice from behind a 'veil of ignorance.' Nozick argued that this procedure violates individual rights — that a fair starting point for political theory must acknowledge entitlements, not erase them. Who has the stronger argument, and what does the answer imply for how we should think about distributive justice?

A.J. Simmons argued that most people are simply not politically obligated — that neither actual consent, tacit consent, fair play, nor natural duty provides a compelling general account of why citizens must obey their governments. If Simmons is right, what follows? Is 'philosophical anarchism' — accepting no general obligation to obey the law — a coherent position for a citizen of a democratic state?

research.topic: The intellectual history of social contract theory from Hobbes through Rawls
research.content: The intellectual genealogy of social contract theory — from its 17th-century origins through its 20th-century revival — has been documented by scholars including Patrick Riley (Will and Political Legitimacy), Michael Lessnoff (Social Contract), and David Boucher and Paul Kelly (The Social Contract from Hobbes to Rawls). The relationship between the historical contract tradition and Rawls's hypothetical contract revival has been analyzed extensively. The consensus: Rawls transformed social contract theory from a historical argument about the origins of political authority into a philosophical method for deriving principles of justice — a transformation that preserved the form while changing the substance.

research.topic: Applications of social contract theory to global justice and climate change
research.content: The extension of social contract thinking to questions of global justice — who owes what to whom across national borders — has generated substantial philosophical literature. Pogge's World Poverty and Human Rights, Beitz's Political Theory and International Relations, and Nussbaum's work on capabilities have all engaged with whether Rawlsian contractualism should extend globally. The application of social contract thinking to climate change — as an intergenerational justice problem — is a more recent development, with theorists including Stephen Gardiner and Henry Shue working on what principles governing emissions and adaptation could be justified to future generations.

research.topic: What the American founders actually understood by 'consent of the governed'
research.content: What evidence would resolve this: systematic evidence about how ordinary citizens in colonial America understood the concept of consent and political authority — whether they applied the philosophical vocabulary of Locke and social contract theory to their own political situation or understood their grievances in different terms. Why partly unavailable: the historical record of ordinary political thinking in colonial America is fragmentary — pamphlets, sermons, and correspondence survive from elite actors but the political thinking of ordinary colonists is less accessible; what survives was produced by people with specific political purposes. What can be established: the elite political discourse of the founding generation, their use of Lockean language, and the specific constitutional structures they created. What cannot be established: how representative that discourse was of ordinary political understanding, or what most colonists thought they were consenting to.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Foundation of Legitimacy
comparativeNarrative.content: The liberal constitutional tradition reads social contract theory as the correct account of political legitimacy — the theory that makes constitutional democracy more than merely factual power. Governments that derive their authority from the consent of the governed, protect the natural or fundamental rights of citizens, and remain accountable to the people through elections and the rule of law are legitimate. Those that don't are not. The American Declaration of Independence and the French Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen are the foundational political documents of this tradition. The social contract is not a historical event but a normative standard against which actual governments are measured.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Excluded Contract
comparativeNarrative.content: The critical tradition — Pateman, Mills, indigenous theorists — reads social contract theory as a theory of inclusion and exclusion: the contract specifies who counts as a full political person and therefore implicitly specifies who doesn't. The historical social contracts that founded liberal democracies excluded women, enslaved people, indigenous people, and the propertyless from full political membership. These exclusions were not accidental failures to apply universal principles consistently — they were constitutive features of the contracts that actually structured political societies. Reforming liberal democracies requires not just extending the contract to excluded groups but rethinking what the contract actually established.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Fictitious Foundation
comparativeNarrative.content: The conservative and communitarian tradition — Hume, Burke, Sandel — reads social contract theory as a philosophical fiction that misrepresents the actual bases of political authority and community. Political societies are not created by contracts among rational individuals; they are inherited communities with traditions, practices, and obligations that predate any individual's choice. The social contract's individualism — its assumption that political authority must be justified to each individual as if they were choosing from scratch — produces a thin and unstable account of political life that cannot ground the thick civic obligations that democratic governance actually requires. The alternative: political authority rests on historical continuity, shared tradition, and the actual practices of political communities.

rabbitHole.label: Hobbes
rabbitHole.reason: Hobbes's Leviathan is the founding text of modern social contract theory and the most rigorous account of why absolute sovereignty is the rational response to the state of nature.

rabbitHole.label: John Locke
rabbitHole.reason: Locke's Second Treatise provides the social contract account that justified the Glorious Revolution and the American Revolution — natural rights, limited government, and the right to dissolve governments that violate them.

rabbitHole.label: Rousseau
rabbitHole.reason: Rousseau's Social Contract provides the most radical social contract account — the general will as the only legitimate basis for political authority, and the requirement that the contract produce genuine political equality rather than merely protecting existing privileges.

rabbitHole.label: John Rawls
rabbitHole.reason: Rawls's revival of social contract theory — transforming it from a historical argument into a philosophical method through the original position and veil of ignorance — is the most important development in the tradition in the 20th century.

rabbitHole.label: Kant
rabbitHole.reason: Kant's political philosophy — deriving political authority from rational consent rather than historical agreement — is the bridge between the historical social contract tradition and Rawls's hypothetical contractualism.

rabbitHole.label: The American Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: The American Revolution was the first large-scale political application of social contract theory — the Declaration of Independence's argument that governments derive their just powers from the consent of the governed and may be dissolved when they violate natural rights is Lockean social contract theory applied to a specific political crisis.

rabbitHole.label: Liberalism
rabbitHole.reason: Social contract theory is the foundational theory of political liberalism — the argument that political authority must be justified to each individual through consent or rational agreement is the liberal tradition's alternative to authority based on tradition, divine right, or force.

rabbitHole.label: Democracy
rabbitHole.reason: The social contract's requirement of consent connects directly to democratic theory — if authority must rest on consent, then regular elections and democratic accountability are the institutional mechanisms through which ongoing consent is expressed and renewed.

reference.author: Hobbes, Thomas
reference.title: Leviathan
reference.annotation: The foundational primary text — Hobbes's state of nature and argument for absolute sovereignty is the starting point of modern social contract theory; the Macpherson or Tuck edition is recommended.

reference.author: Pateman, Carole
reference.title: The Sexual Contract
reference.annotation: The essential feminist critique — Pateman's argument that the social contract established patriarchal power in the private sphere while claiming equality in the public sphere is one of the most important interventions in modern political theory.

reference.author: Mills, Charles W.
reference.title: The Racial Contract
reference.annotation: The essential racial critique — Mills's argument that the actual social contract of Western liberal societies is a racial contract among white people to maintain racial hierarchy is the most important extension of Pateman's analysis.

reference.author: Scanlon, T.M.
reference.title: What We Owe to Each Other
reference.annotation: The essential contemporary contractualist account — Scanlon's grounding of morality in what no one could reasonably reject is the most influential recent development of the social contract tradition in moral philosophy.

reference.author: Simmons, A.J.
reference.title: Moral Principles and Political Obligations
reference.annotation: The essential treatment of political obligation — Simmons's argument that most people are simply not politically obligated is the most rigorous engagement with the social contract's foundational problem; essential for serious philosophical engagement.

commerce.title: Leviathan
commerce.author: Thomas Hobbes (ed. C.B. Macpherson)
commerce.note: Start with the source — Hobbes's state of nature argument is one of the most powerful in political philosophy; Parts I and II are essential; the Macpherson Penguin edition is the most accessible.

commerce.title: The Sexual Contract
commerce.author: Carole Pateman
commerce.note: The essential critique — Pateman's argument that the social contract established patriarchal power while claiming universal freedom is the most important challenge to the liberal tradition from within; essential for any serious engagement.

commerce.title: The Racial Contract
commerce.author: Charles W. Mills
commerce.note: The essential companion — Mills's extension of Pateman's analysis to race is equally important and more accessible; short, sharp, and genuinely disturbing.

commerce.title: What We Owe to Each Other
commerce.author: T.M. Scanlon
commerce.note: The best contemporary contractualism — Scanlon's account of morality as what no one could reasonably reject is the most rigorous recent development of social contract thinking in moral philosophy.

commerce.title: Justice: What's the Right Thing to Do?
commerce.author: Michael Sandel
commerce.note: The most accessible introduction — Sandel's Harvard lectures place social contract theory alongside utilitarianism and virtue ethics; the best starting point for readers with no prior philosophy background.

popularCulture.title: The Declaration of Independence (1776)
popularCulture.description: Jefferson's Declaration — 'all men are created equal, Governments are instituted among Men, deriving their just powers from the consent of the governed' — is social contract theory compressed into a founding document and a declaration of war. Locke's natural rights, consent of the governed, and right of dissolution are all there. The document's power comes partly from the gap between its universal language and its application: 'all men' did not include Jefferson's enslaved people.

popularCulture.title: The Dark Knight Rises — Christopher Nolan (2012)
popularCulture.description: Nolan's conclusion to his Batman trilogy stages a Hobbesian political nightmare: Bane's takeover of Gotham — which begins by releasing prisoners and promising liberation but collapses into terror — enacts Hobbes's argument that the alternative to authoritarian order is not freedom but chaos. Batman's restoration of order represents a version of the Hobbesian social contract — peace through force — that the film simultaneously defends and unsettles.

popularCulture.title: The Good Place — Michael Schur (2016–2020)
popularCulture.description: Schur's afterlife comedy is the most successful popular-culture engagement with academic ethics in television history. The show introduced Scanlon's What We Owe to Each Other to a mass audience — sales of the book spiked after the show began including it. Whether this represents genuine philosophical education or the domestication of serious thought into entertainment is itself a philosophical question the show would appreciate being asked.

popularCulture.title: The Rawlsian classroom exercise
popularCulture.description: The 'veil of ignorance' has become one of the most widely used teaching exercises in political philosophy worldwide. Students are asked to imagine choosing the rules of their society without knowing their place in it. Whatever its limitations, it is one of the most productive thought experiments ever designed for teaching political philosophy.

popularCulture.title: Lord of the Flies — William Golding (1954)
popularCulture.description: Golding's novel — English schoolboys stranded on an island descend from organized society into savage violence — is the most widely read literary staging of the Hobbesian state of nature. Its premises have been challenged by Rutger Bregman, who found a real case: six Tongan teenagers stranded in 1965 who cooperated and helped each other for 15 months until rescue. Whether Golding or the Tongan teenagers better represent human nature without authority is contested.
